narrative:
First Warn Forecast
Thursday, June 11, 2026
Headlines
Scattered strong to severe thunderstorms are expected this afternoon and evening, with damaging winds, large hail, and tornadoes possible.
Another warm and humid day is expected before cooler and less humid air arrives tonight and Friday.
A low chance for additional strong to severe thunderstorms returns Saturday.

After several days of summer-like heat and humidity, one final round of active weather is expected across Missouri and Illinois today. Temperatures will once again climb into the upper 80s and lower 90s this afternoon with muggy conditions remaining in place ahead of an approaching cold front.

The primary weather concern today will be the potential for severe thunderstorms developing along and ahead of the advancing front. The atmosphere will become increasingly unstable during the afternoon, providing favorable conditions for thunderstorms to rapidly intensify. Scattered storms are expected to develop across northern Missouri during the early afternoon before moving southeast through the evening.

The greatest threat for severe weather will be across northeast Missouri and west-central Illinois, generally along and north of Highway 36 and Interstate 72. In this area, storms may develop as discrete supercells before evolving into clusters or line segments. These storms will be capable of producing all modes of severe weather, including damaging wind gusts, large hail, and tornadoes. A few tornadoes could become strong if storms remain isolated ahead of the cold front.

Farther south across the St. Louis metro, southeast Missouri, and southern Illinois, severe thunderstorms remain possible, although the primary threat will shift toward damaging straight-line winds as storms become more organized into clusters and lines. Large hail and an isolated tornado cannot be ruled out.

One factor that will determine the overall severity of today's outbreak is how long storms can remain discrete before merging together. If storms remain isolated longer, the tornado and large hail threat would increase. If storms quickly organize into lines, damaging winds would become the dominant concern.

Thunderstorms will gradually move southeast through the evening and weaken overnight as the cold front exits the region. By Friday, a much more comfortable air mass will arrive with lower humidity, sunshine, and temperatures returning closer to seasonal averages.

Looking ahead to the weekend, another weather system is expected to approach the region on Saturday. While confidence remains lower regarding storm coverage and intensity, there is a low chance that a few thunderstorms could become strong to severe, especially if sufficient moisture and instability return ahead of the next cold front. This potential will continue to be monitored over the coming days.

Today is a day to stay weather aware. Have multiple ways to receive warnings, especially if you have outdoor plans this afternoon or evening. Conditions may change quickly once thunderstorms begin to develop.

First. Accurate. Trusted.
